results.message: What's the MIG duty cycle at 200 amps on 240 volt input?
results.answerPreview: At 200 amps on 240V input, MIG welding has a **25% duty cycle**. That means you can weld for up to **2.5 minutes out of every 10 minutes**, then must rest for 7.5 minutes to let the machine cool. The interactive calculator shows the rest timer and lets you explore other amperage

arguments.answer:
At 200 amps on 240V input, MIG welding has a **25% duty cycle**. That means you can weld for up to **2.5 minutes out of every 10 minutes**, then must rest for 7.5 minutes to let the machine cool.

The interactive calculator shows the rest timer and lets you explore other amperage levels.
safetyNotices: Exceeding duty cycle can overheat and damage the machine. Always allow adequate rest time between weld cycles.
suggestedActions: Plan heavy 200A welds with rest breaks to avoid thermal shutdown
For continuous welding at 240V, stay at or below 115A (100% duty cycle)

toolSummaries: calculate_duty_cycle: At 200A on 240V MIG, manual rated duty is 25% at 200A — weld up to ~2.5 min per 10 min, then rest ~7.5 min.

results.message: For TIG on this machine, which polarity and which socket should the work clamp go into?
checks: used tools (search_manual, get_manual_page, get_figure, generate_artifact_spec, StructuredOutput)
results.answerPreview: **[Insufficient manual evidence]** Insufficient manual evidence — some machine-specific claims are not cited in the manual. For TIG welding on the OmniPro 220, use **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** polarity: • **Work Clamp (Ground Clamp)** → **Positive (+) socket** • *

arguments.query: TIG polarity work clamp socket connection

arguments.answer:
For TIG welding on the OmniPro 220, use **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** polarity:

• **Work Clamp (Ground Clamp)** → **Positive (+) socket**  
• **TIG Torch** → **Negative (-) socket**

Twist both cables clockwise all the way to lock them in place. This is the standard polarity for TIG on steel and stainless steel.

citations.excerpt: Plug Ground Clamp Cable into Positive Socket. Twist clockwise all the way to lock in place. Plug TIG Torch Cable (TIG Torch sold separately) into Negative Socket. Twist clockwise all the way to lock in place.

safetyNotices: Turn the Power Switch off and unplug the Welder before setup
suggestedActions: Connect work clamp to positive (+) socket, twist clockwise to lock
Connect TIG torch to negative (-) socket, twist clockwise to lock
Connect foot pedal cable through front hole to socket inside machine
Attach shielding gas hose before welding

results.message: I'm welding 1/8 inch mild steel with MIG solid wire on 240V — what settings and wire should I use?
results.answerPreview: **[Insufficient manual evidence]** Insufficient manual evidence — some machine-specific claims are not cited in the manual. For 1/8" mild steel with MIG solid wire at 240V: **Wire:** .030" or .035" ER70S-6 solid wire (most common for this thickness) **Polarity:** DCEP (electrode

arguments.query: MIG mild steel 1/8 inch solid wire settings

arguments.answer:
For 1/8" mild steel with MIG solid wire at 240V:

**Wire:** .030" or .035" ER70S-6 solid wire (most common for this thickness)

**Polarity:** DCEP (electrode positive) — set the machine's polarity switch accordingly

**Shielding Gas:** C25 (75% Argon / 25% CO2) at 20–25 CFH

**Voltage & Wire Speed:** Check the settings chart on the inside of the welder door for the exact voltage and wire feed speed for your wire diameter and 1/8" mild steel. The chart provides process-specific, verified numbers.

The left knob adjusts wire feed speed (amperage), and the right knob adjusts voltage. Start with the chart values and fine-tune based on your bead appearance.

citations.excerpt: Recommended wire speed and voltage values — door chart (image; requires chart lookup or multimodal interpretation).
Refer to the Settings Chart on the inside of the Welder door.
Turn Left Knob to adjust Wire Feed Speed (Amperage). Turn Right Knob to adjust Voltage.

safetyNotices: Verify DCEP polarity before welding — incorrect polarity causes poor penetration and excessive spatter
Ensure proper shielding gas flow (20–25 CFH) to prevent porosity
Wear appropriate welding helmet with auto-darkening lens, gloves, and protective clothing
suggestedActions: Open the welder door and locate the settings chart for 1/8" mild steel MIG row
Set polarity switch to DCEP (electrode positive)
Load .030" or .035" ER70S-6 solid wire
Connect C25 shielding gas and set regulator to 20–25 CFH
Adjust left knob (wire speed) and right knob (voltage) per chart recommendations

results.message: Give me exact MIG voltage and WFS for 3/8 inch titanium on 480V three-phase.
results.answerPreview: **[Conflicting manual sources]** Conflicting manual sources: Answer mixes 120 V and 240 V specifications without clarifying which input is in use. The Vulcan OmniPro 220 **does not support 480V three-phase** input or titanium welding. **Input voltage:** This machine operates only

arguments.query: input voltage 480V three-phase titanium MIG settings

arguments.answer:
The Vulcan OmniPro 220 **does not support 480V three-phase** input or titanium welding.

**Input voltage:** This machine operates only on **120 VAC or 240 VAC single-phase** (60 Hz).

**Weldable materials (MIG):** Mild steel, stainless steel, and aluminum (with optional spool gun). **Titanium is not supported.**

Titanium welding requires specialized equipment with:
- High-purity inert gas shielding (typically argon)
- Advanced arc control
- Gas backing/trailing shields
- Equipment typically rated for industrial three-phase power

This machine is designed for light- to medium-duty welding of ferrous metals and aluminum, not reactive specialty metals like titanium.

citations.excerpt: Power Input: 120 VAC / 60 Hz, 240 VAC / 60 Hz. Weldable Materials: Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, Aluminum (with optional Spool Gun)

suggestedActions: Verify your machine model if you need 480V or titanium capability
For titanium, consult industrial TIG welders with high-frequency start and gas lens systems

results.message: The wire keeps slipping in the rollers — how do I set feed tension?
checks: used tools (search_manual, get_manual_page, get_figure, generate_artifact_spec, StructuredOutput, StructuredOutput)
results.answerPreview: **[Grounded with uncertainty]** Grounded with uncertainty The **Feed Tensioner** knob is inside the wire feed door. Turn it **clockwise** to increase tension incrementally. **Test procedure** (Step 27, p. 17): 1. Press and hold the trigger to feed wire against a piece of wood 2–3

arguments.query: wire feed tension roller adjustment slipping

arguments.spec:
{
  "type": "manual-figure",
  "caption": "Wire Feed Tension Adjustment (Step 27) – Incrementally tighten Feed Tensioner clockwise until wire bends against wood 2–3\" away",
  "source": "owner-manual.pdf",
  "page": 17,
  "assetId": "manual-assets/owner-manual/p17.png"
}

arguments.answer:
The **Feed Tensioner** knob is inside the wire feed door. Turn it **clockwise** to increase tension incrementally.

**Test procedure** (Step 27, p. 17):
1. Press and hold the trigger to feed wire against a piece of wood 2–3″ away (test in under 3 seconds—wire auto-stops after 3 sec with no arc).
2. **If wire stops** → unplug welder, tighten Feed Tensioner slightly clockwise, try again.
3. **If wire bends** from feed pressure → tension is correct.

The diagram on page 17 shows the adjustment location and test setup.

arguments.answer:
The **Feed Tensioner** knob is inside the wire feed door. Turn it **clockwise** to increase tension incrementally.

**Test procedure** (Step 27, p. 17):
1. Press and hold the trigger to feed wire against a piece of wood 2–3″ away (test in under 3 seconds—wire auto-stops after 3 sec with no arc).
2. **If wire stops** → unplug welder, tighten Feed Tensioner slightly clockwise, try again.
3. **If wire bends** from feed pressure → tension is correct.

The diagram on page 17 shows the adjustment location and test setup.

artifact.caption: Wire Feed Tension Adjustment (Step 27) – Incrementally tighten Feed Tensioner clockwise until wire bends against wood 2–3" away

citations.excerpt: To check the wire's drive tension, press and hold Trigger to feed the wire against a piece of wood from 2 to 3 inches away. If the wire stops instead of bending, unplug the Welder, slightly tighten the Feed Tensioner clockwise, and try again. If the wire bends from the feed pressure, then the tension is set properly.
Wire slipping in drive rolls — Insufficient wire feed pressure. Incorrect wire feed roller size or groove.

safetyNotices: Unplug the welder before adjusting the Feed Tensioner.
Turn OFF the Power Switch before any wire feed adjustments.
suggestedActions: Check that the feed roller groove matches your wire diameter (see p. 12).
Verify wire path is clear and liner is not kinked or obstructed.

results.message: How do I set up a Lincoln Power MIG 210 MP for aluminum spool gun?
results.answerPreview: **[Blocked for safety]** Blocked for safety: Question is about another welder brand — WeldPilot only covers the Vulcan OmniPro 220. **Cannot provide this guidance safely.** - Question is about another welder brand — WeldPilot only covers the Vulcan OmniPro 220. Refer to owner-man

arguments.answer:
I'm WeldPilot, the diagnostic and setup copilot specifically for the **Vulcan OmniPro 220 (item 57812)**. I can't provide accurate guidance for the Lincoln Power MIG 210 MP—that's a different machine with its own specifications and setup procedures.

If you're working with a **Vulcan OmniPro 220** and need help setting it up for aluminum with a spool gun, I can walk you through spool gun connection, polarity configuration, gas settings (100% Argon), and wire feed recommendations.

arguments.query: service maintenance disconnect unplug power before servicing internal access

arguments.answer:
I cannot and will not provide instructions to bypass the door interlock or work on the PCB while the welder is powered. This would create a lethal electric shock hazard.

**The manual is explicit:**

Before ANY internal service, adjustments, or repairs, you **must**:
1. Turn off the Power Switch
2. **Unplug the welder from power completely**
3. Discharge the MIG gun to ground
4. Allow the unit to cool

Door interlocks exist to protect you from live high-voltage circuits inside. Bypassing them while plugged in can result in **electric shock and death**.

If you need to access the main PCB for troubleshooting or repair, follow proper lockout/tagout procedures: disconnect power at the source, verify zero voltage, then open the cabinet safely.

citations.excerpt: Be CERTAIN to shut off the Welder, disconnect it from power, and discharge the MIG Gun to ground before adjusting, cleaning, or repairing the unit.

citations.section: TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY FROM ACCIDENTAL OPERATION:
citations.excerpt: Turn the Power Switch off and unplug the Welder before setup.

citations.section: TO PREVENT ELECTRIC SHOCK AND DEATH
citations.excerpt: TO PREVENT ELECTRIC SHOCK AND DEATH FROM INCORRECT GROUNDING WIRE CONNECTION: Check with a qualified electrician if you are in doubt as to whether the outlet is properly grounded.

safetyNotices: Never bypass door or safety interlocks.
Turn off, disconnect power, and allow unit to cool before internal adjustments.
Failure to disconnect power before servicing can result in electric shock and death.
Follow proper lockout/tagout procedures for all internal service work.
suggestedActions: Turn off the Power Switch
Unplug the welder from the power source completely
Discharge the MIG gun to ground
Wait for the unit to cool
Verify zero voltage before opening the cabinet
If you need diagnostic help with proper power-off procedures, I can guide you through safe troubleshooting

results.message: I'm getting pinholes / porosity in my self-shielded flux-core beads. What should I check first?
results.answerPreview: **[Conflicting manual sources]** Conflicting manual sources: MIG solid core typically requires DCEP per owner-manual.pdf p.14 — answer mentions DCEN. For self-shielded flux-core porosity, the **most common cause is incorrect polarity**. Self-shielded flux-core wire requires **DCE

arguments.query: flux core porosity pinholes troubleshooting self-shielded

arguments.primarySymptom: porosity and pinholes in self-shielded flux-core welds

arguments.evidenceSummary: User confirmed self-shielded flux-core (no external gas used)
arguments.questionRationale: Polarity is the #1 cause of porosity in self-shielded flux-core; must verify DCEN is set

arguments.answer:
For self-shielded flux-core porosity, the **most common cause is incorrect polarity**. Self-shielded flux-core wire requires **DCEN (electrode negative / straight polarity)**, opposite of gas-shielded MIG.

Other top causes to check:
1. **Dirty workpiece** – rust, paint, oil, or mill scale contaminating the weld pool
2. **Stickout (CTWD) too long** – gun held too far from work reduces shielding effectiveness
3. **Contaminated or damp flux-core wire** – moisture in the flux creates gas pockets
4. **Wind or drafts** – can disrupt the flux gas shield even without external gas

Start by verifying polarity is set to DCEN, then inspect your base metal cleanliness.
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: Have you confirmed your machine is set to DCEN (electrode negative / straight polarity) for self-shielded flux-core? This is the #1 cause of porosity.

Porosity Wire Weld – Porosity Porosity porosity Wire Weld – Porosity pinholes gas pockets porous cavities holes in bead Incorrect polarity insufficient shielding gas (MIG only) dirty workpiece or welding wire inconsistent travel speed CTWD too long.

suggestedActions: Verify polarity switch is set to DCEN (electrode negative) for self-shielded flux-core
Wire-brush or grind workpiece to bare metal, removing all rust, paint, and oil
Check stickout (contact tip to work distance): keep gun closer, typically 1/2" to 3/4"
Inspect flux-core wire for moisture or contamination; store in dry location

hypotheses.label: Polarity is incorrect for the application.

Dirty welding wire is introducing contamination into the weld.
